title: Navigating Uncertainty: A Typology of Reflexive Self-Construction Among Generation Z Sophomores in China
abstract: Existing research on Chinese students at elite universities often interprets their experiences through the lens of social reproduction, but limited attention is given to their agentic responses to structural constraints, competitive labor markets, and evolving expectations. This study explored how Generation Z sophomore students at an elite Chinese university construct their selves during a critical transitional stage of undergraduate study. Drawing on Giddens'reflexive project of the self and Yan's relational person, we conducted interviews with 26 students and identified three typologies: the meritocratic self, which is oriented toward academic achievement and familial expectations; the exploratory self, which resists hyper-competition while maintaining minimal engagement; and the contemplative self, which is marked by periods of low-activity states. By highlighting Generation Z-ers' negotiations of institutional norms, neo-familism, and personal aspirations, this study extends research focused on social reproduction and offers nuanced insights into Chinese sophomore students transitions and subjectivities during uncertain times.
authors_text: Yuan, Boya; Pi, Xingcan; Wang, Xiying
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
